old_claim.note: Guild Certification Seals provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Most late-medieval badges and ampullae used cheap tin-lead or lead alloy, so low-melting lead-alloy working is the material capability behind the cast souvenir form.

source_shape.source_locator: The Kunera overview says late-medieval badges and ampullae were often made from cheap tin-lead alloy.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Pilgrim-badge casting depended on cheap tin-lead or lead-alloy material rather than guild certification seal practice.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The replacement moves the edge from a documentary/authentication institution to the material capability used to make the cast badges themselves.

ontology_before: Pilgrim Badge Casting depended on guild certification seals, implying badge manufacture grew out of guild authentication and regulated seal practice.
ontology_after: Pilgrim Badge Casting is scoped to low-cost tin-lead or lead-alloy cast devotional souvenirs, with lead_working carrying the material-production capability.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: pilgrim_badge_casting now depends on lead_working and no longer directly depends on guild_certification_seals.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to guild-certified trade seals rather than pilgrim badges and ampullae.
A dedicated pewter_casting or lead_tin_alloy_badges node is added and should replace the broader lead_working prerequisite.
A source shows the scoped mass-produced pilgrim badges were not normally made from lead or tin-lead alloy.
short_reason: Lead-alloy working is the material basis; guild certification seals were a mismatched institutional edge.
